question_title: Will the UK's public sector net borrowing excluding public sector banks be below £0 in March 2026?
summary: March has NEVER shown a surplus in the entire 33-year ONS record since 1993. This is a structural pattern: March is the last month of the UK fiscal year when departments accelerate spending to use remaining budgets, and no major tax payment deadlines fall in March (January captures self-assessment). The smallest-ever March deficit was £0.8B (2018), and the current fiscal year 2025/26 shows elevated borrowing (Feb 2026 was the 2nd-highest February since 1993, 11-month cumulative deficit at £125.9B). No extraordinary receipts or structural changes are expected. A March 2026 surplus would be entirely unprecedented.

factors.description: March has NEVER shown a surplus in the entire ONS record since 1993 (0/33 years). This is a structural pattern - March is the last month of the UK fiscal year when departments rush to spend remaining budgets.
Current fiscal year 2025/26 shows elevated borrowing: Feb 2026 was 2nd highest February since 1993, cumulative 11-month deficit at £125.9B. The fiscal trajectory is worsening, not improving.

Even the smallest March deficit on record was £0.8B (March 2018), during a period of much lower government spending. Current spending growth is far higher with public services, benefits, and debt interest all elevated.
No known structural change or one-off receipt expected in March 2026 that could flip the pattern (no major asset sales, privatizations, or extraordinary tax receipts announced).

reasoning:
**Forecast: 2% probability (YES)**

The evidence is overwhelming that March 2026 will NOT show a surplus:

- **Zero historical precedent**: March has been a deficit month in all 33 years since ONS began monthly records in 1993
- **Structural reasons**: March is the final month of the UK fiscal year — departments accelerate spending to use remaining budgets, while no major tax deadlines fall in March
- **Current trajectory**: FY 2025/26 borrowing is running at elevated levels (Feb 2026 was the 2nd-highest February on record; 11-month deficit at £125.9B)
- **Minimum ever**: Even the smallest March deficit was £0.8B (2018), meaning the government was still £800M from surplus in the *best* March ever recorded

The 2% probability accounts for extreme tail scenarios (e.g., an unknown massive one-off receipt) while reflecting that a March surplus would be entirely unprecedented.

resolution_criteria: This question resolves to yes if the Office for National Statistics reports public sector net borrowing excluding public sector banks for March 2026 as a negative number. It resolves to no otherwise. The primary source is the ONS release page "Public sector finances, UK: March 2026" at https://www.ons.gov.uk/releases/publicsectorfinancesukmarch2026
fine_print: Resolve using public sector net borrowing excluding public sector banks, the standard headline monthly borrowing measure, for March 2026. A negative value counts as a surplus. Zero does not count as a surplus. Ignore later revisions unless ONS replaces the original release before resolution. Resolve disputes using credible sources per https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions
